stem: In a round-robin tournament, each of the \( [[n]] \) teams plays every other team exactly once. How many games are played in total?
solution: Each game corresponds to an unordered pair of distinct teams, so the number of games is \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} = \frac{[[n]]([[n]]-1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts each game twice, once for each ordering of the two teams; divide by 2.
This includes a team playing itself; only distinct pairs count.
This mistakes the number of teams for the number of games.
This counts unordered triples of teams \( \binom{[[n]]}{3} \), not pairs.
This overcounts the games by the number of teams.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: \( [[n]] \) points are placed in the plane so that no three are collinear. A line segment is drawn between every pair of points. How many line segments are drawn in total?
solution: Each segment corresponds to an unordered pair of the \( [[n]] \) points, so the number of segments is \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} = \frac{[[n]]([[n]]-1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts each segment twice, once from each endpoint's perspective; divide by 2.
This treats each point as connected to itself as well as to the others.
This mistakes the number of points for the number of segments.
This counts unordered triples of points \( \binom{[[n]]}{3} \), not pairs.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This subtracts the number of points for no valid reason.

stem: A convex polygon has \( [[n]] \) vertices. How many diagonals (segments joining two non-adjacent vertices) does it have?
solution: There are \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} \) segments joining pairs of the \( [[n]] \) vertices in total, and exactly \( [[n]] \) of them are the polygon's sides (joining adjacent vertices), not diagonals. So the number of diagonals is \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} - [[n]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL vertex-pairs, forgetting to remove the \( n \) sides of the polygon.
This forgets to divide by 2 in the equivalent diagonal formula \( \frac{n(n-3)}{2} \).
This adds the sides back in instead of subtracting them.
This double counts every pair of vertices instead of choosing them as an unordered pair.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
